demoTitle: Spotting Digital Waste in Code gives you energy

steps.content: What is this session really about?
Digital waste is hidden cost without value. This journey helps you notice it, understand it, and act on it.
What am I about to learn in this session?
You move through four stages: awareness, dialogue, decision frames, and action. The goal is to make waste visible and discussable.
Where does the journey begin?
It starts with a visual cue. Before code, you are invited to notice the act of looking closely.
Freek records rare sightings because what you observe today can matter tomorrow. Measurement turns attention into something useful.

media.caption: Slide 8: Freek as the spotting metaphor.

steps.content: Spotting trains your eyes. You go out early, keep looking, and slowly learn to recognize what deserves attention.

media.caption: Slide 10: learning to notice that one moment.

steps.content: What changes once I start noticing more carefully?
And then it changes. Once your eyes are trained, you stop seeing only beauty and start noticing waste too.

media.caption: Slide 12: the moment before waste becomes visible.

steps.content: What does that moment of recognition actually look like?
Now it is everywhere: plastic by the road, a can beside the path, waste that used to feel invisible.
Why did I not see that sooner?
Why did you miss it before? Habit makes waste disappear into the background.
What else keeps people from seeing it?
Another reason is ownership. If it feels like someone else's problem, it becomes easy to ignore.

media.caption: Slide 16: not mine means easy to ignore.

steps.content: When does ignoring it stop being possible?
And eventually it reaches the point where ignoring it is no longer possible.

media.caption: Slide 17: until you cannot ignore it anymore.

steps.content: When does this become a value discussion?
The shift happens when value becomes visible. Waste looks very different once you ask what something is worth.

media.caption: Slide 18: what is this tree worth?

steps.content: The tree is framed as real value, roughly 150,000 to 250,000 euros. That reframes careless loss as measurable damage.
How does that connect to software?
You bring in electricity maps to make infrastructure consumption feel physical. Compute is not abstract; it runs somewhere real.

media.caption: Slide 21: Electricity Maps grounding the energy discussion.

steps.content: Here the core definition lands: digital waste is cost without value. That cost can be money, energy, capacity, or attention.

media.caption: Slide 23: digital waste equals cost without value.

steps.content: Then you quantify it. Underused workloads burn energy and money, while consolidation can save cost and cut emissions at the same time.
What should I look for in a real system?
Look for three signals: too much capacity with too little use, slowness without full CPU, or one saturated part while the rest waits.
What forms does digital waste usually take?
You meet it as idle resources, structural overcapacity, repeated default settings, or hidden compute that adds no visible value.
Why does this waste stay alive for years?
Because technical debt makes change expensive. Tight coupling, maintenance burden, and fear of breakage keep waste in place.
How do I think beyond just electricity savings?
The question changes. Not only 'how do we save power?' but 'is this workload healthy, efficient, and worth running at all?'
This visual makes the pivot concrete by separating power use from utilization. A system can consume plenty without delivering much.
The follow-up slides reinforce the same point: healthy workloads balance value, efficiency, and operational quality, not just raw activity.
Can a tiny code change really matter that much?
Only 40 lines of code can hide a massive gap in latency, work done, and energy consumed.
The case starts with something recognizable: ThePrimeagen and a story engineers can immediately relate to.
A bug report and a tiny implementation detail become the entry point. That makes the waste feel concrete, not theoretical.

media.caption: Slide 57: OpenJDK issue and case framing.

steps.content: The deck then explains the core idea: reading procfs means opening text and parsing it, while direct clock access is simpler and faster.
What exactly makes the old path expensive?
The visuals show where the work hides: file open, text read, parsing, copies, and formatting. Small code can still create a heavy path.

media.caption: Slide 60: expensive calls in the old path.

code.caption: Old procfs-based path reconstructed from the code slides.

steps.content: The slides name the hotspots explicitly: fopen, fread, and sscanf. That is where extra micro-ops and switching energy pile up.
So what does the better path change?
The better path asks the kernel directly for the right clock. Less parsing, less copying, fewer syscalls, and much better latency.

code.caption: The optimized path shown in the later code slides.

steps.content: How should I treat this code case during the talk?
Check it out later, not now. The code case should sharpen your eye, not pull attention away from your own systems.
How do I turn this insight into backlog items?
Map each waste item to yearly cost, energy or CO2, performance impact, and risk. That is how it becomes backlog-worthy.
Do not pitch implementation details. Frame it as an outcome, like reducing infrastructure cost by consolidating underused workloads.
Start with safe quick wins. Not a platform revolution, but cleanup, consolidation, and right-sizing.
Give it room to happen: reserve sprint capacity or a quarterly target. Otherwise urgent delivery will always win.
No owner means no living backlog item. Add labels, ownership, and a reason to revisit the work.
What happens if teams leave this alone?
If you do nothing, inefficiency keeps running: more cost, more energy use, and more systems slowly gathering dust.
What decision framework should I leave with?
Use three frames: value, cost, and exception. Ask who uses it, what it costs, and whether the trade-off is intentional.
Then choose: remove it, combine it, calibrate it, or consciously accept it with a named owner.
How should I start after this talk?
Start with one application. Ask what you already do, what blocks progress, and which system has the biggest user, compute, or cost impact.
So what should I walk away with?
You can now spot digital waste, discuss workload quality, inspect code differently, and turn that insight into practical action.
